headline: Junior Caminero Just Had the Week of the Season and the Bombers Survived a Scare: Your Week 14 MillerLite BeerLeagueBaseball Recap
subheadline: One rookie third baseman went full video-game mode, the Albuquerque Bombers needed their pitching staff to save them from the Dragons, and the playoff race just got genuinely complicated.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

**Albuquerque Bombers def. Athletic Park Dragons, 5-4**

Here is the thing about the **Athletic Park Dragons** that should terrify their manager heading into the playoff stretch: they are the fantasy equivalent of a team that puts up 35 points and loses 38-35 because their defense gave up six touchdowns. This week against the **Albuquerque Bombers**, the Dragons posted 27 runs (identical to Albuquerque), crushed 7 home runs to the Bombers' 4, drove in 34 runs to Albuquerque's 21, and stole 7 bags to the Bombers' 4. On paper, that is a dominant offensive performance. In practice, it earned them a 5-4 loss.

The reason is the same reason it is always the reason: pitching. The Dragons threw up a 6.17 ERA and a 1.44 WHIP with zero quality starts in 46.2 innings. Zero. The Bombers' staff, meanwhile, posted a 2.48 ERA, a 1.07 WHIP, and 3 quality starts in 54.1 innings. Dansby Swanson had a monster week with 3 HR and 15 RBI for the Dragons (more on that in the stat leaders), and Jose Caballero's 3 stolen bases gave the team legitimate speed. But none of it mattered because their rotation collapsed completely.

The scarier subplot here is structural. The Dragons carry a +2.38 luck delta, second-highest in the league. Their record flatters them. And when they run into a team like the Bombers, who are built to win the categories that actually separate good rosters from great ones, the illusion evaporates. Albuquerque is now 10-3-1. This is not a fluke. This is roster construction paying dividends when it counts.

## The Week's Defining Moment

Junior Caminero. Full stop.

The stat of the week is 7 home runs and 15 RBI from Caminero in a single seven-day stretch, and I am not sure the fantasy baseball internet is giving this the proper respect it deserves. Seven home runs in a week. Fifteen RBI. Eight runs scored. A.500 on-base percentage. That is not a hot streak. That is a video game cheat code being activated in a real-money league during Week 14 of the season, which is approximately the worst possible time to be on the receiving end of it if your name is **General Hospital**.

The final score was 9-1. Nine to one. The **Buttville Skidmarks** led the league this week in home runs (15 total), RBI (35 total), and runs scored (tied for second at 30). Caminero was the engine driving basically all of it. To put the 7 HR, 15 RBI line in context: the entire **General Hospital** roster combined for 8 HR and 27 RBI, and they actually put up a 2.42 team ERA (second-lowest in the league this week, for what it is worth). General Hospital pitched reasonably well and still lost by eight categories. That is how catastrophically one-sided the offensive gap was.

The larger story here is what this means for the Skidmarks going forward. They came into this week at 5-6-3, which had them firmly in the "interesting but not really a contender" tier. A 9-1 demolition, combined with Caminero's emergence as a genuine weekly difference-maker, changes the conversation entirely. If Parker Messick and Kyle Bradish give them rotation stability down the stretch, this team could be the most dangerous late-season riser in the league. The playoff picture just got one legitimate new entrant.

## Matchups

### Notable: Abbotsford-Colby Twins def. Sugar Land Skeeters, 6-3

Let me tell you about the **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** pitching staff this week, because it deserves its own paragraph in the season's highlight reel. Foster Griffin threw 18 strikeouts with a 0.63 ERA. Jesus Luzardo threw 19 strikeouts with a 1.54 ERA. Together, those two accounted for a combined 37 strikeouts and an ERA that would make most starters weep with envy. The Twins led the entire league this week in ERA (2.30), innings pitched (82.1), quality starts (8), and runs scored (32). That is not winning one or two categories on pitching. That is a complete and total stranglehold on everything a fantasy pitching staff can offer in a given week.

The **Sugar Land Skeeters** were not embarrassed, for what it is worth. Michael Wacha posted a 1.23 ERA with 12 strikeouts, Chase Burns added 10 K, and the team finished second in the league in strikeouts this week with 76. But they could only muster 36 hits in 197 at-bats and a.275 OBP from the lineup, and when your offense is that quiet, it does not matter how well your starters pitch. The Twins move up to 8-6-0 and are quietly putting together a serious case for themselves as a genuine contender if this offense ever wakes up.

### Notable: Canberra Wombats def. Benchwarmers, 6-3

This one deserves attention because of what the **Canberra Wombats** actually did this week. Yandy Diaz posted a.536 OBP. Kumar Rocker struck out 14 batters with a 1.64 ERA. The team ERA was 2.63. And yet the Wombats are sitting at 1-9-4 on the season, carrying the worst luck delta in the entire league at -2.23. This week was genuinely competitive. The **Benchwarmers** had Sonny Gray throw a generational line (20 K, 0.63 ERA, 0.77 WHIP), but their offense posted just 2 home runs and a.286 OBP. Canberra won on the strength of their pitching and OBP. A win is a win, but the Wombats' season record remains one of the great tragedies of this league's 2024 campaign.

### Notable: Riverside Roughnecks def. North Correa, 6-4

**North Correa** had an elite offensive week. Miguel Vargas went 3 HR, 8 RBI. Mookie Betts went 3 HR, 8 RBI. Shohei Ohtani scored 6 runs with a.345 OBP. And they still lost 6-4. That is the **Riverside Roughnecks** in a nutshell: Hunter Goodman (4 HR, 8 RBI) and Ryan O'Hearn (2 HR, 9 RBI) gave the offense a genuine punch, and the team matched North Correa's 30 runs while posting better pitching numbers. The Roughnecks' +2.69 luck delta (highest in the league) keeps getting raised as a red flag, and it is a real concern, but winning close games is still winning.

### Quick Takes

**TIE: BaseballPro77 5, Hiroshima Radiation 5.** Both teams had the horses to win this one outright and neither pulled the trigger. Hiroshima posted 82 strikeouts and 6 quality starts this week and settled for a draw. BaseballPro77 had Jeremy Pena's 3 stolen bases and Gavin Williams' 14 strikeouts and also settled for a draw. Someone has to be more disappointed here, and I think it is **Hiroshima Radiation** (down to #4 in power rankings) because of how dominant their pitching looked on paper.

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers def. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs, 7-2.** The **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** posted a 3.12 ERA and a 0.98 WHIP (lowest in the league this week) and lost by five categories. The reason: 34.2 innings pitched total, which means they simply could not accumulate enough volume to compete. The **Reapers** won batting categories, survived a 5.61 ERA from their own rotation, and kept rolling. Zero quality starts from the Reapers' pitching staff in a win tells you everything about the sustainability problem lurking in their future.

## Standings Watch

The playoff picture heading into Week 15 has three distinct tiers, and each one tells a different story about what the next three weeks are going to look like.

At the top, the **Albuquerque Bombers** at 10-3-1 are the class of the league. Their +0.15 luck delta means the record is essentially exactly what they deserve. They are not getting lucky. They are not winning categories they should not be winning. They are simply better than almost everyone else, and the 5-4 scare against the Dragons this week, where they were outgunned offensively across the board and still won on pitching, is the most perfectly distilled example of why. The **Riverside Roughnecks** at 10-3-1 match the Bombers in record but carry that +2.69 luck delta, which is a flashing warning sign. They will face **BaseballPro77** in Week 15, and that matchup may tell us a great deal about whether the Roughnecks are a legitimate title contender or a team whose record has been running on borrowed time.

**BaseballPro77** at 9-4-1 is right there in the conversation, and the tie with **Hiroshima Radiation** this week was the kind of result that could loom large in the final standings. Both teams feel like they are operating slightly below their ceilings. Hiroshima at 6-5-3 has the most frustrating profile in the league: a -0.77 luck delta suggests they are genuinely underperforming, and 82 strikeouts and 6 quality starts from their pitching this week in a tie game is the kind of result that makes you want to flip a table.

The most interesting movement at the bubble is the **Buttville Skidmarks** at 5-6-3. They were easy to dismiss two weeks ago. After Caminero's historic week and a 9-1 win, they feel like a different team. The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** at 8-6-0 are also climbing, and their pitching staff's performance this week was genuinely elite-level. If this offense produces, they could make a real run.

The **Athletic Park Dragons** at 8-5-1 sit fourth in the standings but deserve serious skepticism given their +2.38 luck delta. Their actual roster quality may place them closer to the 7-9 range in terms of expected wins.

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Athletic Park Dragons.** How does a team post a 6.17 ERA, zero quality starts, and a 1.44 WHIP from their pitching staff and still nearly steal a win? Offense, mostly. Dansby Swanson's 3 HR and 15 RBI carried the lineup, and Jose Caballero's 3 stolen bases added speed, and the Dragons' offense matched the Bombers in runs scored on the week. But the larger issue is that the Dragons' season luck delta of +2.38 is the second-highest in the league. Their record is actively misleading people. At some point, probably in a playoff game, the pitching is going to crater again, and there will not be enough Dansby Swanson heroics to bail them out. The bill is coming due.

**Unlucky Team: Canberra Wombats.** I want to spend a paragraph defending the Wombats because their season record (1-9-4) is genuinely one of the cruelest stat lines in this league's history. This week: Yandy Diaz posted a.536 OBP. Kumar Rocker struck out 14 with a 1.64 ERA. The team ERA was 2.63. They won this week, which is something. But the -2.23 luck delta, worst in the entire league, tells you that this team has been systematically scheduled against the wrong opponents at the wrong times all season. The talent is real. The record is a lie. And if this team somehow misses the playoffs while the Dragons (with their inflated luck number) sneak in, it will be the defining injustice of the 2024 MillerLite BeerLeagueBaseball season.

## Waiver Wire

No notable waiver wire moves were reported this week.

## Trade Value Watch

**Junior Caminero (Buttville Skidmarks): BUY.** Caminero posted 7 HR, 15 RBI, 8 runs, and a.500 OBP in a single week, which is the kind of performance that makes opposing managers immediately check his ownership status and start drafting trade offers. The verdict here is Buy, aggressively, if you can get him from the Skidmarks manager before they realize what they have. If you already own him, Hold forever. Do not let anyone talk you into trading this player right now.

**Dansby Swanson (Athletic Park Dragons): SELL.** Three home runs and 15 RBI in a week is a spectacular line, full stop. But Swanson's value is at its absolute ceiling right now, and the Dragons' structural problems (historically bad pitching, inflated luck delta, zero quality starts this week) mean this team's floor is lower than it looks. If you can extract a premium asset from a contender desperate for a middle infield upgrade, this is the week to make that call.

**Foster Griffin (Abbotsford-Colby Twins): HOLD.** Eighteen strikeouts and a 0.63 ERA in a single week is the kind of performance that generates immediate trade interest from half the league. The Twins' pitching duo of Griffin and Jesus Luzardo is legitimate, but Griffin's value is high enough right now that a Hold makes more sense than selling into the hype unless someone offers you something truly ridiculous. If the offer is ridiculous, take it.

## Power Rankings

1. **Albuquerque Bombers** | Survived a 5-4 scare against the Dragons despite being outgunned offensively. The pitching staff's 2.48 ERA, 1.07 WHIP, and 3 QS won the key categories, and with a luck delta of just +0.15, this team's record is earned. The most complete roster in the league entering the playoff push. [SAME]

2. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Junior Caminero's 7 HR and 15 RBI in a single week is arguably the most dominant individual offensive performance of the season, and a 9-1 demolition of General Hospital vaults this team into legitimate title contention. If Parker Messick and Kyle Bradish stabilize the rotation, this team is dangerous. [UP]

3. **BaseballPro77** | A 5-5 tie with Hiroshima in the week's marquee matchup is a missed opportunity, but Gavin Williams' 14 K, Ranger Suarez's 1.50 ERA, and Jeremy Pena's 3 SB show a well-rounded roster. A luck delta of +1.27 means the record is slightly padded, but the talent is real. [SAME]

4. **Hiroshima Radiation** | Bryce Harper (2 HR, 7 RBI), Curtis Mead (3 HR, 5 RBI), and Drew Rasmussen's 1.50 ERA were excellent, but tying BaseballPro77 when you had 82 K and 6 QS from your pitching staff stings. A luck delta of -0.77 suggests this team is slightly underperforming its potential. [DOWN]

5. **Riverside Roughnecks** | Hunter Goodman (4 HR, 8 RBI) and Ryan O'Hearn (2 HR, 9 RBI) gave this offense a genuine power-and-production punch in a 6-4 win over North Correa, and Jacob Misiorowski's 1.50 ERA is promising from the backend. The luck delta of +2.69 (highest in the league) remains the biggest red flag on an otherwise dangerous roster. [SAME]

6. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Foster Griffin (18 K, 0.63 ERA) and Jesus Luzardo (19 K, 1.54 ERA) were a historically dominant pitching duo in a 6-3 win over the Skeeters, and with 82.1 IP and 8 QS this team led the league in pitching volume. The offense needs to produce more consistently to make this a true contender. [UP]

7. **North Correa** | Miguel Vargas (3 HR, 8 RBI), Mookie Betts (3 HR, 8 RBI), and Shohei Ohtani (6 runs,.345 OBP) gave this team an elite offensive week, but a 6-4 loss to the Roughnecks despite those numbers reveals persistent pitching fragility. The luck delta is nearly neutral at +0.23, meaning this record is an honest reflection. [SAME]

8. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | Michael Wacha (12 K, 1.23 ERA) and Chase Burns (10 K) anchored a solid pitching week, but the offense's 36 hits in 197 ABs and a.275 OBP in a 6-3 loss to the Twins shows the lineup continues to underperform. A luck delta of -1.00 means this team deserves better than its record suggests. [DOWN]

9. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Kazuma Okamoto (3 HR, 8 RBI) and Trea Turner's speed were genuine contributors in a 7-2 win over the Wonderdogs, but zero quality starts and a 5.61 ERA from the pitching staff is deeply concerning. This team wins batting categories and hopes the opponent's pitching collapses, which is not a sustainable playoff strategy. [SAME]

10. **Athletic Park Dragons** | Dansby Swanson's 3 HR and 15 RBI were monster numbers and Jose Caballero's 3 SB gave the team speed, but a 6.17 ERA and zero quality starts in a 5-4 loss to the Bombers confirms that pitching remains an existential problem. The luck delta of +2.38 means the standings are actively lying about where this team stands. [DOWN]

11. **Benchwarmers** | Sonny Gray's 20 K, 0.63 ERA, and 0.77 WHIP was one of the most dominant individual pitching performances of the season, and the 6-3 loss to the Wombats is a tough result, but 2 HR and a.286 OBP from the offense remain chronic concerns heading into the playoff stretch. [UP]

12. **General Hospital** | Andy Pages' 7 runs and Manny Machado's 2 HR were bright spots, but a 9-1 obliteration at the hands of Buttville (with only 1 quality start and 43 IP from the rotation) exposes a team that simply cannot compete in pitching categories. A luck delta of -1.15 suggests they deserve a slightly better record, but the talent gap is real. [SAME]

13. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | William Contreras (2 HR,.455 OBP) and Brooks Lee (2 HR) had solid weeks, and the team's 0.98 WHIP is legitimately elite, but only 34.2 IP and a 7-2 loss to the Reapers despite a better ERA tells the story of a team that cannot generate enough pitching volume to win consistently. A luck delta of -2.00 makes this the most snakebitten roster in the league. [SAME]

14. **Canberra Wombats** | Yandy Diaz's.536 OBP, Kumar Rocker's 14 K and 1.64 ERA, and a team 2.63 ERA all pointed to a competitive week, but only 48 IP and 4 QS from the staff limited their ability to win pitching volume categories in a 6-3 loss. With a luck delta of -2.23 (the worst in the league), this team's true talent is being systematically obscured by their scheduling misfortune. [SAME]

## Looking Ahead

Three matchups deserve your full attention in Week 15.

The obvious headliner is **BaseballPro77 vs. Riverside Roughnecks**. Two teams in the top three of the standings, both carrying slightly inflated luck deltas, both coming off weeks where they left something on the table. If the Roughnecks' +2.69 luck number catches up to them against a well-rounded BaseballPro77 roster, the entire upper tier of the standings reshuffles overnight.

The second one is **Albuquerque Bombers vs. Abbotsford-Colby Twins**. The Twins just posted the most dominant pitching week in the league this season. They are the one roster with the pitching infrastructure to genuinely challenge what the Bombers are doing. If the Twins' offense shows up, this could be the week's actual thriller.

And do not sleep on **Buttville Skidmarks vs. North Correa**. Caminero's momentum is real. North Correa had an elite offensive week and still lost. Week 15 is where we find out if the Skidmarks' resurgence is the real thing or just one spectacular player carrying a roster that cannot sustain it. See you on the other side.
